command: WITHHELD_FOR_SAFETY - mshta variant: remote HTA over HTTP(S) invoked from PowerShell
explanation: Illustrates the abuse of a Microsoft-signed script host (mshta) launched by PowerShell to proxy-execute code hosted in a remote HTA. This is characteristic of System Script Proxy Execution while relying solely on native components.
rationale_steps: PowerShell starts a signed Windows script host (mshta.exe), creating a trusted parent-child chain
mshta loads a remote HTA resource, executing script content under a Microsoft-signed process
This pattern often evades naive application control rules that trust signed hosts
Defenders should monitor PowerShell spawning mshta with network destinations and HTA file indicators
Harden via WDAC/AppLocker to block or constrain mshta usage unless explicitly required

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: No concrete command string is provided; the placeholder is not runnable or parseable as-is.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: Using mshta to load a remote HTA from PowerShell is plausible but maps more closely to Signed Binary Proxy Execution: Mshta (T1218.005) than to System Script Proxy Execution (T1216).

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is explicitly withheld, so no actual syntax can be evaluated; only the description of a mshta-based PowerShell invocation is provided, making it a non-runnable fragment.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The described technique—using PowerShell to invoke mshta.exe with a remote HTTP(S) HTA URL—is a well-known and operationally plausible method of System Script Proxy Execution (T1216), leveraging a Microsoft-signed binary to proxy-execute remote code, though mshta is more precisely associated with T1218.005 rather than T1216 specifically.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The provided text is a descriptive placeholder and technique name rather than an executable PowerShell command.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command string is missing the necessary arguments and logic required to perform the proxy execution described in the MITRE technique.
